request.question: What is the name of the river was the plutonium production facility that released radioactive fission products during the "Green Run" established on?

title: Caesium-137
Reprocessed uranium
Roscoe L. Koontz
Fission product yield
Green Run
Iodine-131
Kenneth Nichols
Kamioka Liquid Scintillator Antineutrino Detector
Hanford Site
Nuclear fission product
sentences: Caesium-137 ( Cs , Cs-137), cesium-137, or radiocaesium, is a radioactive isotope of caesium which is formed as one of the more common fission products by the nuclear fission of uranium-235 and other fissionable isotopes in nuclear reactors and nuclear weapons.

It is among the most problematic of the short-to-medium-lifetime fission products because it easily moves and spreads in nature due to the high water solubility of caesium's most common chemical compounds, which are salts.
Reprocessed uranium (RepU) is the uranium recovered from nuclear reprocessing, as done commercially in France, the UK and Japan and by nuclear weapons states' military plutonium production programs.
This uranium actually makes up the bulk of the material separated during reprocessing.
Commercial LWR spent nuclear fuel contains on average (excluding cladding) only four percent plutonium, minor actinides and fission products by weight.
Reuse of reprocessed uranium has not been common because of low prices in the uranium market of recent decades, and because it contains undesirable isotopes of uranium.
Roscoe L. Koontz (December 16, 1922 – May 17, 1997) was an American Health Physicist.

Trained as one of the first health physicists through the first Atomic Energy Health Physics Fellowship Training Program at the University of Rochester, Koontz contributed to the development of practices, instrumentation, and techniques to protect people from ionizing radiation.
His early research focused on problems of neutron dosimetry, toxicology of uranium, plutonium, and fission products.

Most of his career was spent at Atomics International where his key achievements included designing a pinhole gamma ray camera, developing techniques for measuring absolute thermal neutron flux using radioactive indium foils, and helping design and fabricate equipment to automate air and water sampling equipment and radiation activity measuring devices.
Nuclear fission splits a heavy nucleus such as uranium or plutonium into two lighter nuclei, which are called fission products.
Yield refers to the fraction of a fission product produced per fission.
The "Green Run" was a secret U.S. Government release of radioactive fission products on December 2–3, 1949, at the Hanford Site plutonium production facility, located in Eastern Washington.
Radioisotopes released at that time were supposed to be detected by U.S. Air Force reconnaissance.
Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) requests to the U.S. Government have revealed some of the details of the experiment.
Sources cite 5500 to of iodine-131 released, and an even greater amount of xenon-133.
The radiation was distributed over populated areas, and caused the cessation of intentional radioactive releases at Hanford until 1962 when more experiments commenced.
Iodine-131 (I) is an important radioisotope of iodine discovered by Glenn Seaborg and John Livingood in 1938 at the University of California, Berkeley.
It has a radioactive decay half-life of about eight days.
It is associated with nuclear energy, medical diagnostic and treatment procedures, and natural gas production.

It also plays a major role as a radioactive isotope present in nuclear fission products, and was a significant contributor to the health hazards from open-air atomic bomb testing in the 1950s, and from the Chernobyl disaster, as well as being a large fraction of the contamination hazard in the first weeks in the Fukushima nuclear crisis.
This is because I-131 is a major fission product of uranium and plutonium, comprising nearly 3% of the total products of fission (by weight).
See fission product yield for a comparison with other radioactive fission products.
I-131 is also a major fission product of uranium-233, produced from thorium.
Major General Kenneth David "Nick" Nichols (13 November 1907 – 21 February 2000) was a United States Army officer and an engineer.

He worked on the Manhattan Project, which developed the Atomic Bomb during World War II, as Deputy District Engineer to James C. Marshall, and from 13 August 1943 as the District Engineer of the Manhattan Engineer District.
He was responsible for both the uranium production facility at the Clinton Engineer Works at Oak Ridge, Tennessee and the plutonium production facility at Hanford Engineer Works in Washington state.
The Kamioka Liquid Scintillator Antineutrino Detector (KamLAND) is an electron antineutrino detector at the Kamioka Observatory, an underground neutrino detection facility near Toyama, Japan.
The device is situated in a drift mine shaft in the old KamiokaNDE cavity in the Japanese Alps.
The site is surrounded by 53 Japanese commercial nuclear reactors. Nuclear reactors produce electron antineutrinos (formula_1) during the decay of radioactive fission products in the nuclear fuel.
Like the intensity of light from a light bulb or a distant star, the isotropically-emitted formula_1 flux decreases at 1/R per increasing distance R from the reactor.
The device is sensitive up to an estimated 25% of antineutrinos from nuclear reactors that exceed the threshold energy of 1.8 megaelectronvolts (MeV) and thus produces a signal in the detector.
The Hanford Site is a mostly decommissioned nuclear production complex operated by the United States federal government on the Columbia River in the U.S. state of Washington.
The site has been known by many names, including: Hanford Project, Hanford Works, Hanford Engineer Works and Hanford Nuclear Reservation.
Established in 1943 as part of the Manhattan Project in Hanford, south-central Washington, the site was home to the B Reactor, the first full-scale plutonium production reactor in the world.
Plutonium manufactured at the site was used in the first nuclear bomb, tested at the Trinity site, and in Fat Man, the bomb detonated over Nagasaki, Japan.
Nuclear fission products are the atomic fragments left after a large atomic nucleus undergoes nuclear fission.
Typically, a large nucleus like that of uranium fissions by splitting into two smaller nuclei, along with a few neutrons, the release of heat energy (kinetic energy of the nuclei), and gamma rays.
The two smaller nuclei are the "fission products".
(See also Fission products (by element)).

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: What is the name of the river was the plutonium production facility that released radioactive fission products during the "Green Run" established on?
hop1_docs: Green Run | The "Green Run" was a secret U.S. Government release of radioactive fission products on December 2–3, 1949, at the Hanford Site plutonium production facility, located in Eastern Washington.  Radioisotopes released at that time were supposed to be detected by U.S. Air Force reconnaissance.  Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) requests to the U.S. Government have revealed some of the details of the experiment.  Sources cite 5500 to of iodine-131 released, and an even greater amount of xenon-133.  The radiation was distributed over populated areas, and caused the cessation of intentional radioactive releases at Hanford until 1962 when more experiments commenced.

Iodine-131 | Iodine-131 (I) is an important radioisotope of iodine discovered by Glenn Seaborg and John Livingood in 1938 at the University of California, Berkeley.  It has a radioactive decay half-life of about eight days.  It is associated with nuclear energy, medical diagnostic and treatment procedures, and natural gas production.  It also plays a major role as a radioactive isotope present in nuclear fission products, and was a significant contributor to the health hazards from open-air atomic bomb testing in the 1950s, and from the Chernobyl disaster, as well as being a large fraction of the contamination hazard in the first weeks in the Fukushima nuclear crisis.  This is because I-131 is a major fission product of uranium and plutonium, comprising nearly 3% of the total products of fission (by weight).  See fission product yield for a comparison with other radioactive fission products.  I-131 is also a major fission product of uranium-233, produced from thorium.

Neptunium(IV) oxide | Neptunium(IV) oxide, or neptunium dioxide, is a radioactive, olive green cubic crystalline solid with the formula NpO.  It is a common product of plutonium fission, and emits both α- and γ-particles.

Fission Product Pilot Plant | The Fission Product Pilot Plant, building 3515 at Oak Ridge National Laboratory, was built in 1948 to extract radioactive isotopes from liquid radioactive waste.  It was formerly known as the 'ruthenium-106 tank arrangement'.  It is a relatively small facility; the task of extracting radioactive isotopes later took place at a number of specialised buildings nearby.

Fission product yield | Nuclear fission splits a heavy nucleus such as uranium or plutonium into two lighter nuclei, which are called fission products.  Yield refers to the fraction of a fission product produced per fission.

Fission products (by element) | On this page, a discussion of each of the main elements in the fission product mixture from the nuclear fission of an actinide such as uranium or plutonium is set out by element.

Plutonium hexafluoride | Plutonium hexafluoride is the highest fluoride of plutonium, and is of interest for laser enrichment of plutonium, in particular for the production of pure plutonium-239 from irradiated uranium.  This pure plutonium is needed to avoid premature ignition of low-mass nuclear weapon designs by neutrons produced by spontaneous fission of plutonium-240.
response.summary_1:
- Fact: [1] "The 'Green Run' ... December 2–3, 1949, at the Hanford Site plutonium production facility, located in Eastern Washington." (quoted fragment)
- Fact: [1] Green Run released radioisotopes including "iodine-131" and "xenon-133" (paraphrase).
- No passage among [1]–[7] states the river on which the Hanford Site was established — the river name is missing.

Bridge entity and missing/ambiguous facts:
- Bridge entity: Hanford Site (the plutonium production facility referenced in [1]).
- Missing fact: The name of the river on which the Hanford Site (bridge entity) was established is not present in the provided passages (ambiguous/missing).

Prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve the missing fact:
1) Fetch the "Hanford Site" page (lead/infobox and "Location" or "Geography" sections). Extract the exact sentence or infobox field that names the river (search for the string "River" or "Columbia River").
2) If needed, fetch the full "Green Run" article (section describing site/location) to confirm the specific site wording.
3) If any doubt remains, fetch the "Hanford, Washington" or "Hanford Site geography" subsection and the "Columbia River" page to cross-check phrasing and confirm the river name.

No final answer provided because the required river name is not present or unambiguous in the supplied passages.
response.hop2_query: On which river is the Hanford Site established?
hop2_docs: Hanford Site | The Hanford Site is a mostly decommissioned nuclear production complex operated by the United States federal government on the Columbia River in the U.S. state of Washington.  The site has been known by many names, including: Hanford Project, Hanford Works, Hanford Engineer Works and Hanford Nuclear Reservation.  Established in 1943 as part of the Manhattan Project in Hanford, south-central Washington, the site was home to the B Reactor, the first full-scale plutonium production reactor in the world.  Plutonium manufactured at the site was used in the first nuclear bomb, tested at the Trinity site, and in Fat Man, the bomb detonated over Nagasaki, Japan.

Camp Columbia (Hanford) | Camp Columbia or Columbia Camp was a prison labor camp established on the north shore of the Yakima River opening on February 1, 1944 near Horn Rapids.  The camp was operated between February 1944 and October 1947 by Federal Prison Industries to provide labor supporting the Hanford Site.  The camp was used to house "minimum-custody-type improvable male offenders," who had no more than one year to serve.  These were violators of national defense, wartime and military laws.  Included were conscientious objectors, violators of rationing and price support laws, those convicted of espionage, sabotage and sedition and those convicted by military courts martial.  Aliens who failed to register were also in this category but none of them were sent here because the camp was located on the southern edge of the 670 sqmi Hanford Site.

Washington State Route 24 | State Route 24 (SR 24) is a 79.23 mi long state highway in the U.S. state of Washington.  Beginning at an interchange with Interstate 82 (I-82) in Yakima, the highway travels east into the Yakima highlands before turning north at the Hanford Site to cross the Columbia River on the Vernita Bridge.  From the crossing, the highway travels east through the Hanford Reach National Monument and turns north to end at SR 26 in Othello.  The highway was known as Secondary State Highway 11A (SSH 11A) from 1937 to 1964 and was originally routed through the Hanford Site until the 1940s.  The Vernita Bridge was completed in 1965 along with the route to Othello north of the Hanford Reach and paved in the 1970s.

Umtanum Ridge | Umtanum Ridge is a long anticline mountain ridge in Yakima County and Kittitas County in the U.S. state of Washington.  It runs for approximately 55 miles east-southeast from the Cascade Range, through the Yakima Training Center to the edge of the Columbia River at Priest Rapids Dam and Hanford Reach.  The eastern end of Umtanum Ridge enters Hanford Reach National Monument and the Hanford Site.  Umtanum Ridge is paralleled on the north by Manastash Ridge and on the south by Yakima Ridge.  The Yakima River cuts through the ridge at the Umtanum Ridge Water Gap.

Vernita Bridge | The Vernita Bridge is a bridge on Washington State Route 24 over the Columbia River between Grant County and Benton County, located approximately 10 mi south of Desert Aire and Mattawa, Washington.  The bridge is in the Hanford Reach National Monument near the Hanford Site.

Hanford, Washington | Hanford was a small agricultural community in Benton County, Washington, United States.  It was depopulated in 1943 along with the town of White Bluffs in order to make room for the nuclear production facility known as the Hanford Site.  The town was located in what is now the "100F" sector of the site.

Kings River Viaduct | The Kings River Viaduct is a planned series of bridges and embankments carrying California High-Speed Rail over the Kings River in Fresno County and Kings County, California.  The site is between the Fresno and Kings–Tulare stations, roughly 4 mi east of Laton and 8 mi north of Hanford.  The structure is planned to be over 2 mi long because three river channels must be crossed, two of which were created by floods in the 1860s.
response.summary_2: The Hanford Site was established on the Columbia River. Hanford Site is a mostly decommissioned U.S. nuclear production complex on the Columbia River in eastern Washington, established in 1943 as part of the Manhattan Project and home to the B Reactor, the first full-scale plutonium production reactor.
